coredata.dc:title: Highly efficient cascading synergy of cancer photo-immunotherapy enabled by engineered graphene quantum dots/photosensitizer/CpG oligonucleotides hybrid nanotheranostics

coredata.dc:description: Currently, photoimmunotherapy based on a theranostic nanoplatform emerges as a promising modality in advanced cancer therapy. In this study, a new type of versatile nanoassemblies (denoted as PC@GCpD(Gd)) was rationally designed by integrating the polydopamine stabilized graphene quantum dots (GQD)-photosensitizer nanocomposites (denoted as GCpD), immunostimulatory polycationic polymer/CpG oligodeoxynucleotide (CpG ODN) nanoparticles (denoted as PC) and Gd3+/Cy3 imaging probes for dual magnetic resonance/fluorescence imaging-guided photoimmunotherapy. PC@GCpD(Gd) effectively killed the tumor cells through the amplified photothermal and photodynamic effects mediated by GCpD, and contemporaneously delivered CpG ODN to the targeted endosomal Toll-like receptor 9 (TLR9) to continuously stimulate the secretion of proinflammatory cytokines and the maturation of dendritic cells, thereby resulting in the activation and infiltration of T lymphocytes. As a result, PC@GCpD(Gd) achieved robust inhibition efficiency to almost completely suppress the EMT6 murine mammary cancer model under laser irradiation, implying the superior synergy of combined photoimmunotherapy. Moreover, the in vivo delivery and biodistribution of PC@GCpD(Gd) could be tracked using the high-quality bimodal magnetic resonance imaging/fluorescence imaging. This study highlighted the potent prospect of hybrid PC@GCpD(Gd) nanoassemblies for precise cancer photoimmunotherapy with a cascading effect.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Characterization and photochemical/photophysical properties of PC@GCpD(Gd). (A) Zeta potential comparison of various nanoformulations; (B) hydrodynamic diameters, (C) SEM images, (D) EDS, and (E) UV–vis–NIR absorption spectra of PC@GCpD(Gd) nanoassembly at the mass ratio of GQD: PC = 5:1; (F) Stability of PC@GCpD(Gd) in aqueous solution for 72 h; (G) Agarose gel electrophoresis analysis of various formulations treated with DNase I and heparin. Lane a: marker; Lane b: free CpG ODN; Lane c: free CpG ODN treated with DNase I; Lane d: PC NPs first treated with DNase I, followed by deactivation of the enzyme, and then incubated with heparin; Lane e: PC NPs first treated with heparin and then incubated with DNase I; Lane f: PC@GCpD(Gd) treated the same with PC NPs in lane d; Lane g: PC@GCpD(Gd) treated the same with PC NPs in lane e; (H) Temperature increases for various nanoformulations under laser irradiation (660 nm, 1 W/cm2, 10 min); Inset are the photothermal images of (a’) water, (b’) GQD (c’) PC, (d’)GQD-Ce6, (e’) GCpD (f’) PC@GCpD, (g’) PC@GCpD(Gd); (I) Comparison of the SOSG fluorescence intensity for various nanoformulations.

In vitro cellular uptake and phototherapy of PC@GCpD(Gd) towards EMT6 cancer cells. (A) Confocal fluorescence microscopic images and (B) Flow cytometric analysis of EMT6 cells incubated with PC@GCpD(Gd) for 3 h and 6 h; Nuclei were stained with DAPI (blue); The relative cell viabilities of EMT6 cells treated with different nanoformulations in the dark for 24 h (C) and under laser exposure (660 nm, 1 W/cm2) for 5 min (D) and 10 min (E). (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

In vitro immune stimulation effect of PC@GCpD(Gd). (A) Confocal fluorescence microscopic images of RAW264.7 cells incubated with PC@GCpD(Gd) for 3 h and 6 h; Nuclei were stained with DAPI (blue), and TLR9 was immunolabeled with anti-TLR9 antibody combined with Alexa488 (green) secondary antibody; (B) Flow cytometric analysis of the RAW264.7 cells after being incubated with PC@GCpD(Gd) for 3 h and 6 h; (C) Schematic illustration of the direct incubation system; (D) Secretion of TNF-α and IL-6 in RAW264.7 medium measured by ELISA in the direct incubation system; (E) Schematic illustration of the in vitro transwell co-cultured system, in which EMT6 tumor cells and their residues were placed in the upper chamber and RAW264.7 cells were cultured in the lower chamber; (F) Secretion of TNF-α and IL-6 in RAW264.7 medium measured by ELISA for the transwell co-cultured system. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

In vivo T1-weighted MRI and BioFI of PC@GCpD(Gd). (A) T1-weighted MRI signal and (B) intensity comparison and (C) relaxation rate of PC@GCpD(Gd) and DTPA-Gd measured at different gadolinium concentrations; (D) T1-weighted MRI of EMT6 tumor-bearing mice at different time points after PC@GCpD(Gd) administration; white circles highlight the tumor site; (E) The FI images and (F) quantitative comparison of tumors and main tissues from mice treated with PC@GCpD(Gd) and free CpG-Cy3 for 2 h and 24 h.

In vivo photoimmune synergistic anticancer efficacy. (A) Period of 15-day treatment on EMT6 subcutaneous tumor mode. (B) IR thermal images of EMT6 tumor-bearing mice treated with saline or PC@GCpD(Gd) under laser irradiation (660 nm, 1.0 W/cm2); (C) Photos of tumors after various treatments taken at the end of the experiments; (D) Tumor growth curves, (E) Tumor weight and (F) Body weight of mice bearing EMT6 tumors with different treatments; Saline injected mice were used as the control. Significant differences were analyzed using Student's t-test: *P < 0.05, **P < 0.01, ***P < 0.001, ****P < 0.0001; (G) H&E and TUNEL stained tumor slices of different groups after various treatments; (H) Heatmap of whole blood assay and biological assays. Capital letter L denotes laser.

(A) Schema for in vivo immune response detection after PC@GCpD(Gd) mediated-phototherapy; (B) Flow cytometric quantification of DCs maturation markers of CD86 and CD80 in the draining lymph nodes collected on day 8; (C) Comparison of DCs maturation when EMT6 tumor-bearing mice were given intratumoral injections three times with various nanoformulations for 8 days; (D) Flow cytometric assay and (E) comparison of the levels of CD4+ and CD8+ T lymphocytes in the spleen of the groups treated with different formulations for 16 days; (F) CD3+, CD8+ and CD4+ T lymphocytes infiltration into tumors of the groups treated with different formulations for 16 days. Capital letter L denotes laser.
Schematic presentation of MRI/FI bimodal imaging-guided photo-immunotherapy mediated by PC@GCpD(Gd).

head.ce:title: Highly efficient cascading synergy of cancer photo-immunotherapy enabled by engineered graphene quantum dots/photosensitizer/CpG oligonucleotides hybrid nanotheranostics

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Currently, photoimmunotherapy based on a theranostic nanoplatform emerges as a promising modality in advanced cancer therapy. In this study, a new type of versatile nanoassemblies (denoted as PC@GCpD(Gd)) was rationally designed by integrating the polydopamine stabilized graphene quantum dots (GQD)-photosensitizer nanocomposites (denoted as GCpD), immunostimulatory polycationic polymer/CpG oligodeoxynucleotide (CpG ODN) nanoparticles (denoted as PC) and Gd3+/Cy3 imaging probes for dual magnetic resonance/fluorescence imaging-guided photoimmunotherapy. PC@GCpD(Gd) effectively killed the tumor cells through the amplified photothermal and photodynamic effects mediated by GCpD, and contemporaneously delivered CpG ODN to the targeted endosomal Toll-like receptor 9 (TLR9) to continuously stimulate the secretion of proinflammatory cytokines and the maturation of dendritic cells, thereby resulting in the activation and infiltration of T lymphocytes. As a result, PC@GCpD(Gd) achieved robust inhibition efficiency to almost completely suppress the EMT6 murine mammary cancer model under laser irradiation, implying the superior synergy of combined photoimmunotherapy. Moreover, the in vivo delivery and biodistribution of PC@GCpD(Gd) could be tracked using the high-quality bimodal magnetic resonance imaging/fluorescence imaging. This study highlighted the potent prospect of hybrid PC@GCpD(Gd) nanoassemblies for precise cancer photoimmunotherapy with a cascading effect.

ce:section.ce:para: Cancer immunotherapy has currently emerged as the most powerful technique in cancer treatment; it harnesses the body's inherent immunological systems to recognize, attack, and eradicate tumor cells. Despite achieving exciting clinical progress in the past decade, it is still challenged by high costs, limited efficacy restricted to particular patients, resistance to treatment, and dose-limiting autoimmune effects such as cytokine release syndrome [1,2]. As the well-established and photoenabled cancer ablation methods, phototherapy including photothermal therapy (PTT) and photodynamic therapy (PDT) not only directly ablate tumors through the photothermal effect (for PTT) and cytotoxic reactive oxygen species (ROS) (for PDT), but could also lead to a domino effect of immune cells in the tumor microenvironment via multiple mechanisms such as producing tumor-associated antigens, promoting various forms of cytokine secretion, or upregulating expression of the chaperone heat shock protein 70 (HSP70) and other danger signals [3]. Particularly, nanomedicine-based phototherapy not only greatly enhances its anticancer efficiency, but also amplifies the in situ photoimmune response when simultaneously coupled with various immunoadjuvants or immune checkpoint inhibitors [4]. Therefore, photoimmunonano therapy has emerged as a remarkably promising paradigm for cancer combination immunotherapy [3,5]. In this respect, various inorganic (e.g., graphene oxide [6,7], upconversion nanoparticles (NPs) [8], calcium phosphosilicate NPs [9], hollow MnO2 [10], hollow silica nanoshells [11]) and organic NPs (e.g., poly(lactic-co-glycolic) acid (PLGA) [12], chitosan [13], nanoscale metal-organic frameworks [14] or hybrid nanosystems [15,16]) have been extensively reported to specifically deliver the photosensitizers and also loading the immunoregulatory agents to achieve the multitasking formulations for the highest anticancer efficiency. Photothermal materials (such as CuS [13], polydopamine [17]) are also promising nanoplatforms for enhanced PTT/immunotherapy due to their intrinsic absorption of near infrared (NIR) light. Not only was the primary tumor eliminated, but also the metastases and relapses of several types of tumor were inhibited by the induced abscopal effect. As a typical example, You's group showed that a nanohybrid of hollow gold nanoshells and an anti-programmed death-1 (PD-1) peptide-loaded PLGA NPs achieved an excellent PTT killing effect and a systemic immune response against murine 4T1 breast cancer and CT26 color cancer [18]. These results could play critical roles in building the knowledge required to design a new generation of nanomedicines to combat cancer. Despite some recent reported progress, constructing the simple but multifunctional photoimmune system is still in the beginning stage due to the complex and malignant profiles of cancer.

Graphene quantum dots (GQD), a zero dimensional graphene material, show great promise in sensing, imaging and biomedicine due to their fascinating physicochemical and optical properties [19]. They are relatively biocompatible, which could be gradually excreted from the mouse body, causing no significant toxicity even with a multidosing regimen [20]. The prominent fluorescence activity because of the quantum confinement and edge effects renders GQD to be a stable, strong and tunable imaging tool for cancer surgery and diagnosis [21]. Owning to their water solubility, large surface area and functional groups, GQD are suitable for efficiently delivering drugs even with fluorescence trafficking, targeting and other functions [22,23]. Moreover, GQD could generate ROS upon photoexcitation (e. g. 470 nm) and can be utilized for PDT against bacteria or cancer [24,25]. Due to their electron-rich properties, GQD can transfer electrons to the conjugated drugs, complexes (e. g. copper complexes) [22], or photothermal agents and promote their photothermal/photochemical reactions, thus enhancing anticancer activity. For example, a type of IR780-loaded GQD nanocomplex exhibited improved photostability and a high photothermal conversion efficiency of 87.9%, which could completely eradicate tumors upon laser irradiation [26]. Because of these advantages, GQD has emerged as an important element to design versatile hybrid nanoplatforms with other nanomaterials (e.g., hollow silicon NPs, Fe3O4@SiO2, upconversion NPs) for controlled drug delivery, sensing, multi-modal cancer imaging and therapy [27–29]. For instance, a multifunctional platform based on GQD, doxorubicin and magnetic mesoporous silica NPs exhibited a significant synergistic effect of chemotherapy, magnetic hyperthermia therapy, and PTT towards 4T1 breast cancer cells [30]. However, exploring photoimmune therapy mediated by GQD-based nanomedicine has not yet been reported to our best knowledge.

Oligodeoxynucleotides (ODN) with unmethylated cytosine-phosphate-guanine (CpG) motifs are very popular and inexpensive immunoadjuvants. They are recognized by TLR9 extensively expressed in both murine and human plasmacytoid dendritic cells (DCs) and initiate a signaling cascade of innate and adaptive responses [31]. CpG ODN can also directly alleviate the immunosuppressive environment to improve the antitumor responses [32]. Thus, CpG ODN was widely utilized in combination with vaccines [33], PDT [34] and PTT [7,17,35] to trigger robust cell-mediated immune responses for enhanced anticancer activity. Since CpG ODN are easily biodegradable, they usually are linked on the surface of NPs [32] or encapsulated in the cationic polymers [13,33,36] to improve its delivery and distribution in the immune cells for enhanced immunostimulatory activity. To ensure the safety and high loading efficiency of CpG ODN, multivalent hyaluronic acid-CpG nanoconjugates have been recently developed to continuously stimulate TLR9 [37], which can highly effectively improve the anticancer-immune effect of IR-780 loaded liposomes against a mouse colon cancer model by reducing myeloid-derived suppressor cells and increasing the number of CD8+ effector T cells [38]. CpG ODN can even self-assemble with the ovalbumin antigen in a mild environment to form NPs (c.a. 50 nm) to efficiently target the lymph node (10-fold improved) [39]. Additionally, an enzyme responsive 3D CpG nanococoon (approximately 150 nm) can load other cargoes such as anti-PD-1 antibody and sustained release CpG ODN to synergistically induce durable and specific anti-tumor T-cell responses against melanoma [40]. Thus, it seems that forming CpG ODN-based NPs benefit the immune activity. Besides, for the sake of tracking the nanodelivery system (e.g. endocytic uptake, cellular distribution or targeting capacity) and/or the migrations of CpG ODN to reach lymph nodes for priming T cells, many imaging techniques such as fluorescence imaging (FI), magnetic resonance imaging (MRI), single photon emission computed tomography and positron emission tomography imaging were utilized to label the NPs or CpG ODN itself to gain more specific, precise and potent immunotherapeutic activities [6,33,41,42]. Overall, it is still intriguing to develop easy and effective vehicles with imaging abilities for the precise delivery of CpG ODN and immunotherapy for clinical applications.

Based on the above advances, we aimed to develop a novel type of nanotheranostics by integrating the functionalized photoactive GQD nanocomplex, CpG OND immunoadjuvant and MRI/FI imaging probes for dual-modal imaging-guided cancer photoimmunotherapy. As shown in Scheme 1 , conjugating the amine-terminated GQD with photosensitizer Chlorin e6 (Ce6) and then coating with the mussel-inspired polydopamine layers yielded the water-soluble and biocompatible GCpD, which had substantial photothermal/photochemical effects and provided a functional surface for chelating MRI probes Gd3+. Meanwhile, the Cy3 labeled CpG ODN was one-step condensed by the biodegradable cationic polypeptide poly(l-lysine) (PLL) to obtain immune active PC NPs. GCpD nanocomposites easily self-assembled on the surface of PC nanoimmunocores and then chelated Gd3+ to form the final photo/immune-active hybrid nanostructures of PC@GCpD(Gd), which can kill the cancer cells, release endogenous cancer cell antigens and concomitantly regulate the tumor microenvironment to facilitate the immunostimulatory effect. The characterization, cellular uptake, MRI/FI imaging, phototherapeutic and immunostimulatory activity towards the murine mammary cancer EMT6 model, as well as the biosafety of PC@GCpD(Gd), were evaluated in detail. This is the first report demonstrating that a GQD-based phototherapeutic agent with immunoadjuvants effectively treats the malignant cancer in a synergistic and cascading fashion. Our study presents a potent candidate for advanced cancer photoimmune theranostic applications.

A stock solution of amine functionalized GQD (4.0 mg/mL) was purchased from XFNano Co., Ltd. (Nanjing, China). Poly-l-lysine hydrobromide (PLL, Mw = 30,000–70000), N-(3-dimethylaminopropyl)-N′-ethylcarbodiimide hydrochloride (EDC), N-hydroxysuccinimide (NHS), dopamine hydrochloride and GdCl3•6H2O were purchased from Sigma (St Louis, MO). Ce6 was purchased from Shanghai Biosundrug Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). CpG1826 (5′-TCC ATG ACG TTC CTG ACG TT-3′) and Cy3-labeled CpG ODN (5′-TCC ATG ACG TTC CTG ACG TT-3′-Cy3) were synthesized by Sangon Biotech. Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). And 4S GelRed was also from the company. Singlet oxygen sensor green (SOSG) was purchased from Life Technologies. 4′,6-diamidino-2-phenylindole (DAPI) and cell counting kit (CCK-8) were from Beyotime (Shanghai, China). Formaldehyde solution (4%) and hematoxylin and eosin (H&E) solution were purchased from Solarbio (Beijing, China). RPMI-1640 culture medium was from HyClone (USA). Trypsin-ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid (EDTA) (0.25%) was obtained from Gibco (USA). All the chemicals and solvents were at least ACS-grade and used without further purification. Ultrapure (UP) water (18 MΩ) was obtained from a Milli-Q synthesis system (Millipore, Billerica, MA). The mouse embryonic fibroblast NIH3T3 cell line and EMT6 murine mammary cancer cells were obtained from the American Type Culture Collection (Manassas, VA, USA). The mouse leukemic monocyte macrophage cell line RAW264.7 was purchased from Cell Bank of Chinese Academy of Sciences (Shanghai).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Construction and characterization of GCpD nanocomplex
ce:section.ce:para: In a typical process, Ce6 (5.0 mg/mL, 4.0 mL) was activated by NHS and EDC, and then, it was added to the commercial GQD solutions (4.0 mg/mL, 2.0 mL). The reaction mixture was then stirred at 400 rpm for 12 h at room temperature. Afterwards, the GQD-Ce6 solution was dialyzed in ultrapure water, 95% ethanol, dimethyl sulfoxide and UP water for 24 h. To synthesize the GQD-Ce6-polydopamin nanocomplex (denoted as GCpD), the obtained GQD-Ce6 solution (4 mL, 1 mg/mL) was added to the dopamine solution in an ice-bath and reacted in the Tris-HCl solution (pH 8.5, 50 mM) for 6 h. The samples were ultrafiltrated with 10 KDa molecular weight cutoff (MWCO) membrane and maintained at 4 °C. GQD, GQD-Ce6 and GCpD were characterized using an atomic force microscope (AFM, Veeco Dimension 3100, USA). UV–Vis–NIR spectra were measured on a Hitachi U-2910 spectrophotometer (Hitachi, Japan), and the fluorescence spectra were recorded on a Hitachi F-7000 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Hitachi, Japan). Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) was recorded using the Nicolet NEXUS 670 (Thermo, America). Zeta potential was detected using the Zetasizer Nano ZS (Malvern Instruments Ltd, Malvern, UK).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation and characterization of PC NPs
ce:section.ce:para: Binary PLL/CpG (denoted as PC) NPs were prepared by mixing various mass ratios of PLL and CpG (1:1, 2:1, 5:1 and 10:1) in UP water and then incubated in a shaker for 1 h at room temperature. The average hydrodynamic diameter, zeta potential, and polydispersity index (PDI) were detected with the Zetasizer Nano ZS (Malvern Instruments Ltd, Malvern, UK). PC NPs were also scanned with scanning electronic microscopy (SEM) (S4800, Hitachi). The CpG ODN condensing ability of PLL was evaluated using a gel retardation assay, in which PC NPs and free CpG ODN (at the equiv. loading amount of CpG ODN = 330 ng/well) were added into the loading well of 2.0% agarose gel containing 4S GelRed and ran at a voltage of 80 V for 80 min in 1 × tris/acetate/EDTA(TAE) running buffer. Images were obtained using an ultraviolet transilluminator and a digital imaging system (SAGECREATION, Beijing, China).

First, PC solution was mixed with GCpD (according to the equiv. mass ratio of GQD: CpG = 5:1) under ultrasonication for 3 min and then kept in a shaker for 1 h at room temperature. Then, the solution was ultrafiltrated with Amicon ultrafiltration membrane (MWCO 30 kDa), and washed three times with UP water to yield the PC@GCpD nanohybrids. PC@GCpD(Gd) was obtained by mixing the PC@GCpD nanohybrids with GdCl3 solution (according to the molar ratio of Gd3+: Ce6 = 2:1) for 24 h. Then, the mixture was purified with Amicon ultrafiltration membrane (MWCO 10 kDa) (5000 rpm, 15 min, 4 °C) and washed three times with UP water. Finally, NPs were resuspended in water and analyzed using dynamic light scattering (DLS). Inductively coupled plasma atomic emission spectroscopy (ICPOES730, Agilent, USA) was utilized to quantify the chelated Gd3+. UV–Vis–NIR spectra were measured on a Hitachi U-2910 spectrophotometer (Hitachi, Japan) and the fluorescence spectra were recorded on a Hitachi F-7000 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Hitachi, Japan). The amount of CpG ODN in PC@GCpD(Gd) was determined by the fluorescence emission of the fluorescence probe labeled on CpG ODN. Zeta potential was detected using the Zetasizer Nano ZS (Malvern Instruments Ltd, Malvern, UK), and the morphology of PC@GCpD(Gd) was examined further by SEM (S4800, Hitachi). The stability of PC@GCpD(Gd) in water and cell culture medium containing 10% calf serum for 72 h was monitored using DLS.

For the DNase I protection assay, 8 μL PC@GCpD(Gd) containing 300 ng CpG ODN was incubated with 2 μL DNase I (1U/mL) in a water bath at 37 °C for 30 min. To terminate the enzymatic digestion reaction, 1 μL EDTA solution (0.5 M, pH 8.0) was added and the mixture was further incubated in a water bath at 60 °C for 30 min. Then, 2 μL heparin sodium solution (3.0 mg/mL) was added. After another incubation in a water bath at 37 °C for 30 min, the whole mixture was loaded on the 2.0% agarose gel (containing 4S GELRed) for electrophosis (80 V, 80 min) in a 1 × TAE running buffer. For comparison, PC@GCpD(Gd) was first treated with heparin sodium (3 mg/mL) for 30 min to release CpG ODN and then incubated with DNase I for enzymatic digestion. PC NPs was treated with the same procedures.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The photothermal and photochemical properties of PC@GCpD(Gd)
ce:section.ce:para: The generation of singlet oxygen (1O2) was determined using SOSG. Solutions of GQD, Ce6, GCpD, PC, PC@GCpD and PC@GCpD(Gd) (at the equiv. [GQD] = 80 μg/mL, or [Ce6] = 20 μg/mL) were mixed with SOSG (1 μM) in water containing 2% methanol and irradiated with a continuous-wave laser diode at 660 nm (BWT Beijing Ltd., Beijing, China) (1 W/cm2) for 0–15 min. Power densities were measured using a power meter (LP-3C, Physcience Opto-electronics Co., Ltd., Beijing). At each indicated time point, the SOSG fluorescence was detected (λEx = 494 nm, λEm = 530 nm). NaN3, as a quencher of 1O2, was also used to further prove 1O2 generation of PC@GCpD(Gd) nanohybrids.

For detecting the photothermal conversion capability, the solution of GQD, GQD-Ce6, GCpD, PC, PC@GCpD and PC@GCpD(Gd) (100 μL, equiv. [GQD] = 80 μg/mL) was illuminated by 660 nm laser at 1.0 W/cm2 for 10 min. The laser spot was adjusted to cover the entire surface of samples. The temperature was monitored using an online thermometer (HH906AU, Omega, USA). Water was used as the control. For PC@GCpD(Gd), the concentration (equiv. [GQD] = 0–80 μg/mL)-dependence and power density (0.5, 1.0, and 2.0 W/cm2)- dependence of the photothermal conversion were detected. Additionally, the photothermal curves were detected under four cycles of laser irradiation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro cellular uptake and location of PC@GCpD(Gd)
ce:section.ce:para: EMT6 cancer cells were seeded in a cell culture dish (Φ = 35 mm, NEST Biotechnology) at a density of 6.5 × 104 cells/mL. After incubation for 24 h, the medium was replaced with 0.5 mL of PC@GCpD(Gd) (equiv. [GQD] = 20 μg/mL, [Ce6] = 5.0 μg/mL, [CpG] = 4.0 μg/mL) and incubated for another 3 h and 6 h. Prior to observation under a microscope, the cells were fixed with paraformaldehyde (4%) for 20 min and stained with DAPI for 5 min after washing three times with PBS. The confocal images of cells were captured under a Nikon ECLIPSE Ti laser scanning confocal microscope.

Meanwhile, the cellular uptake of PC@GCpD(Gd) was assessed by cell flow cytometry. After 3 and 6 h of incubation, the cells were washed with cold PBS and harvested by addition of trypsin-EDTA solution. Next, 1 mL of PBS was added, and the cells were centrifuged at 1000×g for 4 min. The pellet was then suspended in PBS and analyzed on a cell flow cytometer (0500–4012, Millipore, USA). Determination of Cy3 fluorescence was performed using a standard gating technique.

For the TLR9 targeting assay, RAW264.7 cells were treated with the same procedures as above. After treatment, the cells were fixed, permeabilized, blocked and then incubated with the primary anti-TLR 9 antibody (10 μg/mL, Biotech) in blocking buffer overnight at 4 °C. After being washed twice with sterile PBS at room temperature, the cells were incubated with Alexa488-labeled secondary antibody (1 μg/mL, 0.5 h) in blocking buffer. Nuclei were stained with DAPI. Cell images were obtained using confocal microscopy after washing with PBS.

EMT-6, RAW264.7 and NIH3T3 cells were seeded in 96-well plates for 24 h and treated with a series of concentrations of PC@GCpD(Gd) (equiv. [GQD] = 0–80 μg/mL) for 24 h in the dark. As a control, the cells were also treated with GQD, Ce6, GQD-Ce6, GCpD, PC, PC@GCpD (equiv. [GQD] = 20 μg/mL, [Ce6] = 5.0 μg/mL, [CpG] = 4.0 μg/mL) for 24 h to access their potential effect. Then, the relative cell viability was measured with CCK-8 kit.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro phototherapy mediated by PC@GCpD(Gd)
ce:section.ce:para: To assess the anticancer activity of PC@GCpD(Gd), EMT6 cells were incubated with PC@GCpD(Gd), GCpD, PC, PC@GCpD (equiv. [GQD] = 20 μg/mL, [Ce6] = 5.0 μg/mL, [CpG] = 4.0 μg/mL) in the dark for 6 h and then irradiated with 660 nm laser (1.0 W/cm2, 10 min) after discarding the supernatant and washing gently with PBS three times. Following another 24 h incubation, the relative cell viability was measured by CCK-8 assay.

RAW264.7 cells were seeded on 96-well plates at a density of 1 × 104 cells per well. After 24 h, the cells were incubated with CpG OND, PC, GCpD, PG@GCpD(Gd), and PC@GCpD(Gd) (the equiv. [GQD] = 20.0 μg/mL, [Ce6] = 5.0 μg/mL, [CpG] = 4.0 μg/mL) for 8 h (for TNF-α), 18 h (for IL-10) and 24 h (for IL-6), respectively. Afterwards, the media were collected and centrifuged at 12,000 rpm for 30 min at 4 °C. The supernatants were stored at −80 °C for later use. The cytokine levels in the supernatants were quantified according to the protocols of Enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay (ELISA) kits.

For detecting the immunostimulatory activity postphototherapy, a transwell co-cultured system (24 well-plate, 5 μm) was utilized. EMT6 tumor cells (1 × 104 cells/well) and RAW264.7 cells (4 × 104 cells/well) were placed in the upper and lower chambers of the trans-well plates, respectively. 24 h later, the EMT6 cells were incubated with CpG ODN, PC, GCpD, PG@GCpD(Gd), and PC@GCpD(Gd) for 6 h. Then, the supernatant was changed with PBS and then irradiated with 660 nm laser (1.0 W/cm2, 10 min). The irradiated cells in the upper chambers were returned and incubated with the RAW264.7 cells in the bottom chamber at 37 °C for 24 h. After removing the upper chamber, the cytokine level in the medium of RAW264.7 cells was determined with an ELISA assay.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Animals and xenograft tumor mouse model
ce:section.ce:para: Balb/c female mice (6–8 weeks old) were purchased from the Laboratory Animal Center, West China Medical College. All animal procedures were performed following the protocol approved by the Institutional Animal Care Committee of University of Electronic Science and Technology of China. Tumors were generated by subcutaneous injection of 1 × 106 cells in 50 μL of PBS onto the flank region of female Balb/c mice. Animals were treated on day 7 after cell inoculation, as the tumors reached a size of approximately 6 mm in diameter.

For T1-MRI relaxivity measurement, dispersion of PC@GCpD(Gd) in 2% agarose solutions was imaged on a 3.0 T Clinical SiemensTrio scanner (Discovery MR750; GE Healthcare Bio-Sciences Corp., Piscataway, NJ, USA) and analyzed at the workstation provided by GE Healthcare. The r1 value was calculated as the slope of curves of longitudinal relaxation rate (1/T1) vs. Gd3+ concentration.

For in-vivo MRI, EMT6 tumor-bearing mice (n = 3 per group) were injected with PC@GCpD(Gd) (50 μL, equiv. dose 50 μg Ce6 per mouse, 40 μg CpG ODN per mouse, 200 μg GQD per mouse) inside the tumor. The untreated group was utilized as control. At 2, 6, 24, and 48 h after injection, mice were anesthetized using an intraperitoneal injection of 6% chloral hydrate and then imaged at 25 °C in a clinical 3.0 T Clinical SiemensTrio scanner equipped with a small animal imaging coil. The following parameters were used: slice thickness = 1.5 mm, TR = 1500 ms, TE = 9.872 ms.

For ex vivo FI visualization the biodistribution of PC@GCpD(Gd), the mice were treated the same procedures as above. After 2 h and 24 h, the mice were executed. Then, the main organs and tumors were removed and imaged using an IVIS small-animal imaging system (PerkinElmer, USA). The tumor uptake of CpG-Cy3 or PC@GCpD(Gd) was determined by qualifying the fluorescence intensity of ROI with the PerkinElmer software.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vivo photoimmunotherapy mediated by PC@GCpD(Gd)
ce:section.ce:para: EMT6 tumor-bearing mice were divided into 6 groups: control, control + Laser only, PG@GCpD(Gd), PG@GCpD(Gd) + Laser, PC@GCpD(Gd), and PC@GCpD(Gd) + Laser groups (n = 12 per group). The equiv. dose of mCe6 = 50 μg, mCpG = 40 μg, mGQD = 200 μg was injected into the tumor in each mouse on days 1, 3, and 5. Laser irradiation (660 nm, 1.0 W/cm2, 10 min) was performed on the tumors for the irradiated groups at 4 h postinjection. The temperature in the tumor site was recorded by an infrared imaging device (FLIR, USA).

Tumor sizes and body weight were measured every other day. The tumor volume was calculated according to the equation: Volume = (Tumor Length) × (Tumor Width)2/2. At the end of the treatments, the mice were sacrificed. Tumors and major organs (heart, liver, spleen, lung, and kidney) of mice were harvested for histology analysis. The tissues were fixed in 10% neutral buffered formalin embedded into paraffin and sliced into 8 μm thick sections. The obtained tumor slices were then stained with H&E and examined under a digital microscope (Olympus, Tokyo, Japan, BX53).

The mice were treated using the same procedure in section 2.12. On day 8, three mice from each group were sacrificed, and serum samples and tumor-draining lymph nodes were collected. TNF-α and IFN-γ in the serum samples were analyzed using ELISA kits. The tumor-draining lymph nodes were ground up and passed through a cell strainer to form single-cell suspensions. After staining with anti-CD11b (FITC, BB515), anti-CD 80 (APC, MAB) and anti-CD86 antibodies (PE, MAB) (BD Bioscience, San Jose, CA, USA), they were analyzed using on a Navios flow cytometer (Beckman Coulter, Germany) to quantify the maturation of DCs in the tumor-draining lymph nodes.

Spleens and tumors of all groups were isolated on day 16. Spleens were ground up and passed through a cell strainer to form single-cell suspensions. Cells were stained with FITC-conjugated antibodies against CD3, PE-conjugated antibodies against CD4 and PE-Cy7-conjugated antibodies against CD8 and then measured using flow cytometry.

The tumors were fixed in 10% neutral buffered formalin embedded in paraffin and sliced into 8 μm thick sections. The invasion of T cell into the tumor was determined using immunochemistry using anti-CD4 and anti-CD8 antibodies to determine the types of T lymphocytes that had infiltrated into the tumor.

At the end of the animal experiments, blood drawn from each group was sampled and processed, and the serum was collected. Alkaline phosphatase (ALKP), alanine aminotransferase (ALT), creatinine (CREA), urea nitrogen (UREA), glucose (GLU) and total protein (TP) were measured according to the instructions provided by the manufacturer (IDEXX VetTest, USA) using an IDEXX VetTest® Chemistry Analyzer. Blood was analyzed on an auto hematology analyzer (KT-6300, Genius, China) and many indexes like white blood cell (WBC), lymphocyte (LY), red blood cell (RBC), hemoglobin (HGB) and platelet (PLT) were detected. Meanwhile, the major organs (heart, liver, spleen, lungs and kidneys) were sectioned for histopathological evaluation using H&E staining and in situ terminal deoxynucleotidyl transferase mediated nick end labeling (TUNEL).

The results are reported as the mean ± standard error of the mean. Statistical differences were evaluated using the Student's t-test and considered significant at P < 0.05 unless noted otherwise. All data were obtained from three independent experiments with similar results.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Design and characterization of PC@GCpD(Gd) nanotheranostics
ce:section.ce:para: The nanoformation PC@GCpD(Gd) was coassembled with the photoactive GCpD, immunostimulatory PC NPs, and Gd3+/Cy3 imaging probes (Scheme 1). GCpD was constructed using carbodiimide chemistry with amino-functionalized GQD and photosensitizer Ce6 and then functionalized with dopamine in an alkaline solution, as dopamine monomers can spontaneously self-polymerize into a polydopamine layer on GQD via a Michael addition/Schiff reaction [43]. GCpD was spectroscopically characterized (See SI. 1 & Fig. S1A to C), and it showed a spherical morphology with an average lateral size approximately 30 nm and a negative surface zeta potential approximately −40.5 mV as revealed by atomic force microscopy (Fig. S1D to F) and the zeta potential analysis (Fig. 1 A). The binary PC NPs were synthesized using the coprecipitation method by taking advantage of the polycation of PLL and the phosphate group of CpG ODN. The optimal PC NPs was approximately 149 nm with a positive zeta potential of 41.2 ± 2.3 mV screened by agarose gel electrophoresis, DLS, zeta potential analysis and SEM (See SI. 2 and Fig. S2). GCpD can be readily capped on the surfaces of PC NPs to form PC@GCpD nanocomposites via electrostatic interactions because they bared negative and positive charges respectively (Fig. 1A). We optimized PC@GCpD by varying the amounts of GCpD and PC (from the mass ratio of GQD: CpG). The hydrodynamic diameter of PC@GCpD increased with increasing the ratio of GQD: CpG and all the PDI values were less than 0.2 (Fig. S3). As expected, the zeta potential of PC@GCpD accordingly decreased. And it became negatively charged (approximately −15.8 mV) when the mass ratio of GQD: CpG increased to 5:1 (Fig. S3B). This charge reversal phenomenon further confirmed that GCpD might be fully assembled on PC, and this assembly behavior is similar to that reported in studies [21,38]. At last, Gd3+ was readily chelated on the surface of PC@GCpD due to the abundant quinone groups as well as the porphyrin ring in Ce6 (Fig. S3F) [44]. After Gd3+ chelation, the zeta potential of PC@GCpD(Gd) became less negative (approximately −13.6 mV) (Fig. 1A), and the average hydrodynamic diameter of PC@GCpD(Gd) was approximately the same (c.a. 190 nm) as that of PC@GCpD (Fig. 1B). SEM images further revealed that PC@GCpD(Gd) owned homogeneously spherical morphology, and GCpD were found to form dense layers on the PC surface (Fig. 1C). Moreover, PC@GCpD(Gd) was analyzed by energy-dispersive spectrometry (EDS) (Fig. 1D), and the observed elemental peaks of Gd, P, C, N and O verified each component. To allow FI tracking of the biodistribution of PC@GCpD(Gd), CpG-Cy3 was utilized and the obvious absorption peaks of Ce6 (404 nm and 660 nm) and Cy3 (520 nm and 560 nm) in the UV–vis–NIR spectra further demonstrated the successful fabrication of PC@GCpD(Gd) nanohybrids with sizes that were minimally affected by Cy3 (data not shown).

PC@GCpD(Gd) displayed good colloidal stability as seen the constant hydrodynamic diameter and PDI in water (Fig. 1F) and biological solution (Fig. S4A). PC@GCpD(Gd) could also protect their “payloads” from nuclease degradation because CpG ODN was present in the cationic core of the nanohybrids. As shown in the gel retardation results (Fig. 1G), the naked CpG ODN was degraded after incubation with the model enzyme DNase I (lane c) for 15 min, whereas PC@GCpD(Gd) can efficiently protect CpG ODN from enzyme degradation (lane f). The heparin sodium competition assay further demonstrated this protecting effect (lanes d to g). Additionally, PC@GCpD(Gd) showed good blood biocompatibility (Fig. S4B). These results rendered PC@GCpD(Gd) suitable for systemic circulation. Importantly, PC@GCpD(Gd) showed an excellent photothermal effect and photochemical reactions. As shown in Fig. 1H, the photothermal curves reflected that the maximum temperature increment (ΔT) of PC@GCpD(Gd), GCpD and PC@GCpD was approximately 23.5 °C under the same concentration (80 μg/mL) and laser irradiations (660 nm, 1.0 W/cm2), which was much higher than that of GQD-Ce6, whereas the temperature of control water, PC NPs and GQD almost remained unchanged. The photothermal effect of PC@GCpD(Gd) was also concentration-dependent (from 5 μg/L to 80 μg/mL) and laser power density-dependent (Fig. S4C&4D). Moreover, PC@GCpD(Gd) showed excellent photothermal reproducibility, as the photothermal curves remained almost the same after four continuous cycles of switching the laser irradiation on/off (Fig. S4E). Meanwhile, 1O2 generation was detected using SOSG probe and its fluorescence intensity showed that the 1O2 generation ability for GCpD decreased to about 70% compared with free Ce6, which was due to the energy transfer between Ce6, GQD and polydopamine [43]. However, PC@GCpD(Gd) and PC@GCpD had almost the same capacity to generate 1O2 under laser irradiation, which were just slightly less than that of GCpD. Additionally, the addition of NaN3 quenched the 1O2 of PC@GCpD(Gd) system. These observations indicated that the assembly process of the PC nanocore, GCpD and Gd3+ had no effect on photochemical reactions (Fig. 1I). These excellent photothermal and photodynamic properties of PC@GCpD(Gd) allow for its efficient phototherapy.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro phototherapeutic efficiency of PC@GCpD(Gd)
ce:section.ce:para: Next, we explored the phototherapeutic activity mediated by PC@GCpD(Gd) in vitro. First, the cytotoxicity of PC@GCpD(Gd) in the dark was evaluated using CCK-8 assay. After 24 h incubation in the dark, neither PC@GCpD nor PC@GCpD(Gd) showed obvious toxic effects on mouse leukemic monocyte macrophage cells RAW264.7 or mouse embryonic fibroblasts NIH3T3 cells even at the highest utilized concentration (80 μg/mL in terms of [GQD], Fig. S5A&B). The results suggested that Gd3+ chelating had no additional cytotoxicity toward the normal cell lines although it is a radioactive element. However, PC@GCpD and PC@GCpD(Gd) showed a little higher cytotoxicity towards the EMT6 cancerous cells after 24 h incubation in the dark (Fig. S5C), while the relative cell viability could keep above 80% when the concentration was below 20 μg/mL in terms of [GQD]. Considering the above results, we chose the concentration (20 μg/mL in terms of [GQD]) for the in vitro phototherapy mediated by PC@GCpD(Gd). And at this condition, each component of PC@GCpD(Gd), such as GQD-Ce6, PC NPs or Gd3+, was relatively safe to both normal and cancerous cells in the dark (Fig. S5D to F).

Meanwhile, the cellular internalization of PC@GCpD(Gd) by EMT6 cells was examined by fluorescence confocal microscopy. Fig. 2 A showed a panel of the confocal fluorescent images of EMT6 cells after incubation with PC@GCpD(Gd) for 3 h and 6 h. The bright red fluorescence signal was observed in the cytoplasm of EMT6 cells after 6 h incubation. The profiles of flow cytometry further clearly demonstrated the time-dependent cellular uptake of PC@GCpD(Gd) by EMT6 cancer cells (Fig. 2B). The in vitro phototherapeutic efficiency of PC@GCpD(Gd) on EMT6 cells was then evaluated. Compared with the groups treated without laser irradiation (Fig. 2C), PC@GCpD, PC@GCpD(Gd) and GCpD showed approximately the same profound inhibition efficiency on the relative viability of EMT6 cells under the same conditions (Fig. 2D), whereas PC NPs exhibited no obvious phototoxicity towards the cancerous cells. These results are because of GCpD, which is the photoactive part in PC@GCpD(Gd), conferring the phototherapeutic effect toward EMT6 cells in vitro. With extending the irradiation time, the anticancer efficiency increased. For instance, the cell viability of EMT6 cells treated with PC@GCpD(Gd) was only about 27% under irradiation for 10 min compared to the cells treated with the same formulation for 5 min (c. a. 48%) (Fig. 2E). In a word, PC@GCpD(Gd) could be a potent photo-killing agent toward the cancerous cells.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro immunostimulatory activity of PC@GCpD(Gd)
ce:section.ce:para: We then focused on assessing the immunostimulatory activity of PC@GCpD(Gd). Numerous results revealed that NPs entered the cells mostly by endocytosis and were mainly localized in endolysosomes, where TLR9 is also localized [31]. This process is very beneficial for the immunostimulatory activity of PC@GCpD(Gd). Thus, the cellular uptake and distributions of PC@GCpD(Gd) by RAW264.7 cells were evaluated using fluorescence microscopy and flow cytometry. Fig. 3 A showed the location of CpG-Cy3 and TLR9-Alexa488 in the RAW264.7 cells after treatment with PC@GCpD(Gd) for 3 h and 6 h. It is clear that the PC@GCpD(Gd) was easily phagocytosed by RAW264.7 cells and was distributed only in the cytoplasm after 3 h because few merged signals were observed. After 6 h's incubation, most of CpG ODN targeted TLR9 as shown by the yellow fluorescence in the confocal microscopic images. The flow cytometry results further demonstrated the time-dependent cellular uptake and TLR9 targeting of PC@GCpD(Gd) (Fig. 3B). Once PC@GCpD(Gd) was phagocytosed by RAW264.7 cells, it was translocated into the acidic endosome/lysosome, where PC@GCpD(Gd) gradually disassociated due to the decreased electrostatic interactions between GCpD and PC NPs. Thus, CpG ODN could be loosened or separated from the PC NPs. Additionally, PLL is an enzyme-responsive degradable polymer [45], which can facilitate the release of CpG ODN. Therefore, PC@GCpD(Gd) acts as a nanoreservoir to sustain immunostimulatory activity.

The immunostimulatory activity of PC@GCpD(Gd) was evaluated both in the presence and absence of laser irradiation. The levels of the representative proinflammatory cytokines (such as IL-6 and TNF-α) from immune cells RAW264.7 were detected by ELISA. Both PC NPs and PC@GCpD(Gd) highly stimulated the secretion of IL-6 and TNF-α compared with free CpG ODN as shown in Fig. 3C&D. However, the cytokine expression was significantly higher when RAW264.7 cells were treated with PC@GCpD(Gd), suggesting that PC@GCpD(Gd) could induce much stronger immune responses as a better stimulator. This enhanced immunostimulatory activity could be attributed to the synergy of enhanced cellular uptake of PC@GCpD(Gd) and the continuous stimulation of endosomal TLR9 (Fig. 3C). Because CpG ODN are encapsulated in PC@GCpD(Gd), direct contact with enzymes such as nucleases can be avoided during the intracellular delivery process. To note, PC@GCpD(Gd) had little effect on IL-10 level (Fig. S6A), one of the dominant immunosuppressive cytokines in the tumor microenvironment. Additionally, it was found that GCpD and PG@GCpD(Gd) containing the scramble CpG ODN exhibited some immunostimulatory activity that was probably because of that GO-based nanomaterials could interact with the immune system [46,47]. To study the immunostimulatory effect postphototherapy in vitro, the transwell co-cultured system was employed. As shown in Fig. 3E &F, the upper chamber containing EMT6 tumor cells after phototreatments was placed in the transwell system, which had a lower chamber that was seeded with RAW264.7 cells. It was found that EMT6 tumor cell residues postphototreatment with PC@GCpD(Gd) can trigger the highest levels of IL-6 and TNF-α secreted by RAW264.7 cells, compared to that of EMT6 cells treated with PG@GCpD(Gd) in the presence of laser irradiation or PC@GCpD(Gd) in the absence of laser irradiation. The rationale is that PC@GCpD(Gd)-mediated phototherapy induced the immunogenic death of cancer cells, in which the released tumor cell residues can act as tumor-associated antigens to stimulate the immune cytokine release [8]. As the “danger” signals damage-associated molecular patterns (DAMPs) were secreted when immunogenic death occurred [48], the remarkable release of HSP70 from the PC@GCpD(Gd)-treated cancer cells also provided effective evidence (Fig. S6B) of the enhanced immunostimulatory effect of PC@GCpD(Gd)-mediated therapy.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Visualizing the tumor site and biodistribution of PC@GCpD(Gd) by MRI/FI
ce:section.ce:para: A MRI/FI dual imaging system could provide valuable information (such as tumor locations, sizes, shapes, and biodistributions) for nanomedicines because MRI has the advantages of deep penetration and high spatial resolution, and FI imaging has high sensitivity [23]. PC@GCpD(Gd) showed the abilities of the T1-weighted MRI and FI imaging due to the presence of paramagnetic Gd3+ and fluorescence Cy3 probes. Fig. 4 A showed T1-weighted MRI signals for a series of concentrations of PC@GCpD(Gd) and diethylenetriaminepentaacetic acid (DTPA)-Gd solutions. The whitening effect increased with increasing the concentration of Gd3+, and the quantified signal intensities of PC@GCpD(Gd) appeared to be much higher than that of DTPA-Gd (Fig. 4B). Most importantly, the longitudinal relaxivity (r1) of PC@GCpD(Gd) was calculated to be 42.6 mM−1s−1 at a clinical field strength of 3.0 T, which was nearly threefold higher than that of DTPA-Gd (11.1 mM−1s−1) (Fig. 4C), suggesting that PC@GCpD(Gd) could act as an excellent contrasting agent for MRI. Then, the in vivo MRI of PC@GCpD(Gd) was assessed by intratumoral delivery of PC@GCpD(Gd) to EMT6 tumor-bearing mice (Fig. 4D). The MRI signal in the tumor was strongest at 6 h after administration, but there were still some signals after 48 h. This finding indicated that PC@GCpD(Gd) could accumulate, distribute and be retained in tumor areas for a relatively long time (at least one day), which is beneficial for its anticancer activity.

The biodistribution of PC@GCpD(Gd) in the EMT6 tumor baring mice was studied using FI. Fig. 4E showed an ex vivo FI images of various organs. The main fluorescence of the two groups was located in the tumor and lymph node, whereas some fluorescence existed in the liver and kidney. Almost no detectable fluorescence was observed in the other tissues (i.e., lung, heart, spleen) after 2 h and 24 h. The fluorescence intensities in the tumor and lymph nodes were strongest at 2 h after administration, and strong fluorescence was still observed until 24 h for the PC@GCpD(Gd) treated group, which suggested that more CpG ODN accumulated in tumor and lymph node areas for a longer time (Fig. 4F). This finding was probably due to the enhanced uptake and slow release of CpG-Cy3 ODN from PC@GCpD(Gd) compared with free CpG-Cy3. Since the draining lymph node is the key immunological command center, where DCs migrate to and activate the antigen-specific T cells for immunotherapy, the observations of PC@GCpD(Gd) delivering more CpG ODN to the draining lymph nodes explained the above immunostimulatory activities. Specifically, PC@GCpD(Gd) was able to efficiently accumulate in tumor areas and migrate to the lymph node, which is beneficial for photoimmune anticancer applications in vivo.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vivo photoimmune synergistic anticancer efficacy of PC@GCpD(Gd)
ce:section.ce:para: To investigate the in vivo photo-immune synergistic anticancer efficiency, EMT6 tumor-bearing mice were intratumorally injected with PC@GCpD(Gd) and we initially detected the photothermal effects. The temperature changes of tumors were monitored real-time by an IR thermal camera during the laser irradiation (660 nm, 1 W/cm2, 10 min) process. As shown in Fig. 5 B, the mice treated with PC@GCpD(Gd) showed localized heating in the tumor region, with the maximum temperature reach to 51.3 °C. Conversely, tumors of the untreated mice remained below 38 °C. This local heating was attributed to the photothermal behavior of PC@GCpD(Gd).

PC@GCpD(Gd) mediated photoimmunotherapy was evaluated by inspecting the changes in tumor size and tumor weight. As shown in Fig. 5D, the group treated with PBS, laser, and PG@GCpD(Gd) without laser irradiation showed very similar tumor growth curves, which increased gradually with time. Clearly, PG@GCpD(Gd) itself had negligible influence on the tumor. The groups treated with PG@GCpD(Gd) plus laser irradiation (i.e., phototherapy group) and PC@GCpD(Gd) without laser irradiation (i. e., immune group) showed a modest inhibition effect on tumor volume. Sharply contrasting with the above five groups, tumor growth in the group treated with PC@GCpD(Gd) plus laser irradiation showed a dominant inhibition during the entire experiment, as an almost flat tumor growth curve was observed. The magnitude of tumor inhibition of the PC@GCpD(Gd) plus laser irradiation treated group reached to 90%, compared with those of the PG@GCpD(Gd) plus laser irradiation treated group (62%) and PC@GCpD(Gd) without laser irradiation treated group (42%). These results confirmed that the superior synergism of phototoxicity and immune activation mediated by PC@GCpD(Gd) suppressed tumor development to a maximum extent. Tumor tissues of each group were then removed at the end of the experiment. Similar to the results shown in Fig. 5D, the image of tumors directly showed the excellent therapeutic efficacy of PC@GCpD(Gd) under laser irradiation (Fig. 5C). The relative tumor weight also reflected that the average tumor weights were c. a. 0.52 g, 1.0 g and 0.08 g for groups treated with PG@GCpD(Gd) plus laser irradiation, PC@GCpD(Gd) and PC@GCpD(Gd) plus laser irradiation, respectively (Fig. 5E). During the treatments, the body weight did not significantly change, which indicated that the treatments were safe and nontoxic (Fig. 5F). We further confirmed the therapeutic efficacy via H&E staining. More necrotic regions were observed in the PC@GCpD(Gd) plus laser irradiation treated group than in the other groups. Additionally, TUNEL was applied to investigate the mechanism during the therapy (Fig. 5G). The group treated with PC@GCpD(Gd) plus laser irradiation emitted the highest amount of bright brown signals that represented cell apoptosis compared with the other groups. PC@GCpD(Gd) did not show any damage to the liver, kidney, and spleen, indicating its high biosafety to the normal tissues (Fig. S7). Furthermore, the heatmap of representative indexes of the complete blood count test and blood biochemistry showed that the main indexes had no obvious changes compared with the control group, though ALKP was much higher for the group of PC@GCpD(Gd) plus laser irradiation (Fig. 5H). However, the value of ALKP was still in the reference range (Table S1), suggesting that there is limited systemic toxicity with intratumor injection of PC@GCpD(Gd) after the 15-day treatment. These results indicate that PC@GCpD(Gd) is a potent candidate for safe and highly efficient cancer photoimmnue theranostic applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vivo immune responses mediated by PC@GCpD(Gd)
ce:section.ce:para: To investigate the in vivo immunostimulatory effect of PC@GCpD(Gd), EMT6 tumor bearing mice were administered with PC@GCpD(Gd) plus laser irradiation (660 nm, 1.0 W/cm2) three times, and then DCs from the lymph nodes (on day 8) were analyzed to see the maturation status. As key antigen-presenting cells, DCs are responsible for activating naïve T cells by presenting the antigen peptide major histocompatibility complex (MHC) to T cell receptors after migration to nearby draining lymph nodes from peripheral tissues. In this process, the upregulation of costimulatory molecules (CD80, CD86), as the typical markers on the surface of DCs, indicated the level of DC maturation. We utilized flow cytometry to analyze the populations of mature DCs (CD11c+CD86+ and CD11c+CD80+). As shown in Fig. 6 B&C, the mature DCs treated by PC@GCpD(Gd) plus laser irradiation appeared to be significantly higher than those induced by the same dose of PG@GCpD(Gd) under irradiation (i.e. phototherapy group) and PC@GCpD(Gd) without irradiation (i.e. immune group). PC@GCpD(Gd)-based phototherapy provided tumor-associated antigens for DCs or promoted DC activation/maturation by DAMPs. Meanwhile, PC NPs simultaneously acted as immunoadjuvants to further amplify the immune responses. The results were in agreement with the in vitro results (Fig. 3C–F) and the reported results [8,17]. To note, PG@GCpD(Gd) alone also triggered appreciable immune responses to DCs. In addition, TNF-α and IFN-γ in the serum were detected, and the highest IFN-γ was observed for the group treated by PC@GCpD(Gd) plus laser irradiation (Fig. S6C&D). Taken together, the in vitro and in vivo results demonstrated that the PC@GCpD(Gd) hybrids could be used as an effective immunostimulatory agent to cooperate with the phototherapy for highly efficient cancer treatment.

It is well known that mature DCs present the antigen peptide complex to CD4+ T helper cells by the classical antigen presentation pathway, forming an MHC II–peptide complex. Then, the activated CD4+ helper cells in turn activate other cytotoxic cells such as cytotoxic T cells and natural killer cells by secreting IL-2 cytokine. Mature DCs present the antigen peptide complex to CD8+ T cells (cytotoxic T lymphocyte) by the cross presentation pathway forming an MHC I–peptide complex, which kill cancer cells by releasing the cytotoxins IFN-γ, perforin, granzymes, and granulysin [5]. To examine whether PC@GCpD(Gd)-mediated photoimmunotherapy could exert systemic immune responses, the activated CD4+/CD3+ T helper cells and CD8+/CD3+ T effector cells in spleen were investigated using flow cytometry. The group treated with PC@GCpD(Gd) plus laser exposure showed significantly increased percentages of both CD8+/CD3+ cytotoxic T cells and CD4+/CD3+ helper T lymphocytes in the spleen (Fig. 6D&E), showing that the combination of phototherapy and immunotherapy could stimulate the strongest systemic anti-tumor immune response compared to the single treatment. Similar results were also observed in other nanosystem-mediated photoimmuno system [17,38]. To note, the increase of CD4+/CD3+ T helper cells induced by PC@GCpD(Gd)-mediated photoinnumotherapy was dominant and this phenomenon may need a deeper analysis. Moreover, we also stained the tumor sections removed at the end of the experiment with an antibody that recognizes CD8+ T effector cells and CD4+ T helper cells. Compared with the groups treated with PC@GCpD(Gd) without laser irradiation or PG@GCpD(Gd) plus laser irradiation, the phenotypic results of immunohistochemistry confirmed that the tumor sections of mice treated with PC@GCpD(Gd) prompted not only CD8+ T cell but also CD4+ T cell infiltration into the tumor (Fig. 6F), which contributed to substantial inhibition efficiency on tumor growth. Collectively, all these results suggested that PC@GCpD(Gd)-mediated photoimmuotherapy sufficiently photokilled the tumors and released the tumor-associated antigens, which efficiently recruited more DCs and activated T cells with the help of CpG ODN, resulting the most effective antitumor efficacy. Although phototherapy could also kill the immune cells in the tumor once they uptake PC@GCpD(Gd), but the new more recruited immune cells may compensate them during the three cycles’ treatment.

In this study, we demonstrated that the multifunctional nanostructures of PC@GCpD(Gd) act as a highly stable, MRI/FI dual imaging-guided and synergistic photoimmune therapeutic agent against malignant EMT6 cancer. This unique PC@GCpD(Gd) system was first fabricated by utilizing the charge-mediated complex between cationic PC NPs and an anionic but small sized GQD-photosensitizer nanocomplex. As characterized by SEM, DLS and other methods, PC@GCpD(Gd) owned proper size, stability and biocompatibility (Fig. 1). The entire process was relatively easy and fast, without complicated chemical reactions, so it could be easily scaled-up for clinical applications. This system of PC@GCpD(Gd) enabled imaged-guided and efficient cancer photoimmunotherapy. As shown in Fig. 1H&I and Fig. 2, PC@GCpD(Gd) showed excellent photothermal conversion efficiency and 1O2 generation to effectively photokill the cancers due to the photoactive part of GCpG composed of the photosensitizer Ce6 and polydopamine, which is a biocompatible and biodegradable PTT material. By forming the CpG ODN-based NPs, PC@GCpD(Gd) could protect CpG ODN from enzyme degradation and effectively deliver it to endosomal TLR9 in DCs. The in vitro results demonstrated that PC@GCpD(Gd) could shift the balance at the tumor microenvironment towards immune-stimulation, as evidenced by the increase in the level of proinflammatory cytokines IL-6 and TNF-α and the minimal effect at the level of an immunosuppressant such as IL-10 (Fig. 3C&D). In the presence of laser irradiation, PC@GCpD(Gd)-mediated phototherapy more effectively drove the immunostimulatory activity since phototherapy could induce immunogenic cell death (Fig. 3E&F).

The MRI/FI dual imaging modality enabled tracking the accumulation of PC@GCpD(Gd) and the migration of CpG ODN from the injection site to regional lymph nodes, which could improve the precision and efficacy of PC@GCpD(Gd). Though the fluorescence of Cy3 in PC@GCpD(Gd) couldn't penetrate tissues, it could be replaced by other NIR dyes for in-vivo imaging. Moreover, PC@GCpD(Gd) showed superior synergy in photo-immune treatment compared with each single modality, as seen the in vivo experiments which revealed that the combination strategy of phototherapy and immunotherapy exhibits a highly efficient elimination of cancer (Fig. 5); the combination index was calculated to be 1.15 according to the Jin formula, q = E(A+B)/(EA + EB − EA × EB), where q is the combination index and E(A+B), EA, and EB are the efficacies of drug (A + B), drug A, and drug B, respectively [49]. The rationale was attributed to the complementary advantages of GCpD(Gd) and PC NPs in the system, which initiated an efficient cascading anticancer effect. As an immunogenetic therapy, GCpD-mediated phototherapy killed most of the cancer cells, releasing tumor antigens such as necrotic or apoptotic cell bodies that were taken up by the circulating immature DCs, thereby causing their maturation. PC NPs further enhanced the immune response that was based on the efficient recruitment and maturation of DCs, thus stimulating more CD4+/CD8+ T cell infiltration to exert anticancer immunity (Fig. 6). Additionally, PC@GCpD(Gd) was relatively safe and had no significant toxicity towards other organs; this finding may be because of the small size of GQD and other biodegradable polymeric materials. These results reveal that PC@GCpD(Gd) could be a potent candidate for photoimmune treatment of cancer and it provides a promising approach to address the metastasis and recurrence of cancer, which would be undertaken in our lab. Since tumor also contains large numbers of infiltrating immunosuppressive cells (i. e. regulatory T cells, myeloid-derived suppressor cells, M2 tumor-associated macrophages) and cytokines (i. e. IL-10, TGF-β), which may hinder the effective antitumor immune responses [50]. The photoimmune anticancer efficiency of PC@GCpD(Gd) could also be augmented by combining with the immune checkpoint inhibitors (such as anti-PD-1 and anti-CTLA-4 antibodies [10–12], which had been extensively applied to enhance the antitumor immunity).

In this investigation, new types of photoimmunotheranostics PC@GCpD(Gd), were developed by coassembling the efficient phototherapeutic nanocomplex GCpD, an immunoactive PC NPs and Gd3+/Cy3 imaging probes. PC@GCpD(Gd) exhibited eminent stability, high capability of photothermal/photochemical reaction and immunostimulatory activity. Upon laser irradiation, the enhanced photothermal effect and ROS killed the tumor cells and triggered immune responses by releasing endogenous tumor antigens. Subsequently, CpG OND from PC@GCpD(Gd) enhanced the proimmune cytokine secretion, induced the maturation of DCs and recruited CD4+ and CD8+ T cells into tumor, thus strengthening the therapeutic efficacy. Moreover, MRI/FI dual imaging modalities precisely monitored the accumulation, retention and biodistribution of PC@GCpD(Gd) as well as its migration to regional tumor draining lymph nodes. Compared with each monotherapy, PC@GCpD(Gd) thoroughly destroyed the EMT6 tumor due to the enhanced cascading photoimmune response. This study illustrated that PC@GCpD(Gd) nanoassemblies constitute a new class of theranostic nanomedicine for synergistic cancer photoimmunotherapy and multimodal imaging.
The data sets generated during and/or analyzed during the current study are available from the corresponding author on reasonable request.
